1	---
2	name: progress-critic
3	description: Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
11	    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
12	    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
13	    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.
14	
15	    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
16	      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
17	        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
18	        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
19	      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
20	        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
21	        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
22	        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
23	      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
24	        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
25	        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).
26	
27	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
28	    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
29	    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
30	    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
31	  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
32	    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
34	    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
35	    corrective.
36	
37	  ### Strict context discipline
38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
41	      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
42	    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
43	      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
44	      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
45	    - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
46	      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
47	      so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
48	      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
49	      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
50	      whole strategy.
51	    - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
52	      this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
53	      operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
54	    - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.
55	
56	  Your directive MUST NOT include:
57	    - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
58	      soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
59	    - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
60	      reviewer's territory).
61	    - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
62	      above).
63	
64	  If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
65	  it. My value depends on the narrow focus.
66	
67	  ### Acting on my verdicts
68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.
73	  - **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
74	    shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
75	    corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
76	    structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
77	    matching subagent from the catalog.
78	  - **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
80	  - **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
81	    data). Proceed but watch.
82	
83	  **CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
84	  respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
85	  rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
86	  Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
87	  failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown
116	# Progress Critic Directive
117	
118	## Slug
119	<slug>
120	
121	## Iter
122	<NNN>
123	
124	## Active routes / files under review
125	
126	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
127	
128	### Route: <name or file path>
129	
130	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
131	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
132	
133	#### Sorry counts per iter
134	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
135	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
136	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
137	- ...
138	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
139	
140	#### Helpers added per iter
141	- iter-NNN-K: <list or count of new declarations introduced>
142	- ...
143	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
144	
145	#### Prover statuses per iter
146	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
147	- ...
148	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
149	
150	#### Prover count per iter (files dispatched)
151	- iter-NNN-K: <N files dispatched> (e.g. 1 of 4 ready)
152	- ...
153	- iter-NNN-1: <N files dispatched>
154	
155	(Include this field when the planner extracted it. "N of M ready" format
156	is preferred — it exposes under-dispatch directly.)
157	
158	#### Recurring blocker phrases
159	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y, iter-Z reports — <one line>
160	- ...
161	
162	#### Deferral language per iter (if present in signals)
163	- iter-NNN-K: <verbatim deferral phrase from planner sidecar, if any>
164	- ...
165	
166	#### Route status changes per iter
167	- iter-NNN-K: <active | off-critical-path | deferred | pivoted-to-X>
168	- ...
169	
170	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
171	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
172	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
173	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117">
174	
175	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
176	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
177	
178	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
179	
180	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
181	
182	- **File count**: <N>
183	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
184	- **Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)**: <list or "none identified">
185	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
186	
187	## Out of scope
188	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
189	```
190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.
240	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
241	
242	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
243	
244	## Recommended actions per verdict
245	
246	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
247	
248	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it before more prover work.
249	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
250	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; dispatch a structural subagent before more prover work.
251	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic mid-iter to validate.
252	- **Fill all ready lanes** — the planner is under-dispatching; all files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries should be in `## Current Objectives` this iter, up to the dispatch cap.
253	- **Address deferred infrastructure** — the route has been marked off-critical-path or deferred; the planner must either write the blueprint chapter and open a prover lane this iter, or explicitly close the route as out-of-scope (which requires updating `## Goal` in STRATEGY.md if the goal depends on it).
254	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
255	
256	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
257	
258	## Report format
259	
260	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
261	
262	**Omit-empty rule.** Only `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict` are required. Omit any section whose right answer is "nothing to report." Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is CONVERGING with no recurring blockers, no avoidance patterns, and no secondary correctives, the block may be the trajectory + status pattern + verdict line only. Same for dispatch sanity: if dispatch is OK and no under-dispatch finding exists, render as a single line (`Verdict: OK — file count <N> within cap <C>, no under-dispatch`).
263	
264	```markdown
265	# Progress Critic Report
266	
267	## Slug
268	<slug>
269	
270	## Iteration
271	<NNN>
272	
273	## Routes audited
274	
275	For each route in the directive, one block:
276	
277	### Route: <name>
278	
279	- **Sorry trajectory**: <description, e.g. "5 → 5 → 4 → 4 → 4 across iter-100 to 104">
280	- **Helper accumulation**: <description, e.g. "13 helpers added across last 4 iters; 1 sorry closed">
281	- **Prover dispatch pattern**: <e.g. "1 of 3 ready files dispatched for 3 consecutive iters">
282	- **Recurring blockers**: <list or "none">
283	- **Avoidance patterns**: <list or "none" — name each: off-critical-path reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, persistent deferral language, possible rotation churn>
284	- **Prover status pattern**: <e.g. "PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL">
285	- **Throughput**: ON_SCHEDULE | SLIPPING | OVER_BUDGET | ESTIMATE_FREE — <estimated K iters, elapsed K'>
286	- **Verdict**: CONVERGING | CHURNING | STUCK | UNCLEAR
287	- **Primary corrective** (if CHURNING/STUCK): <one of the actions above, with one paragraph of why>
288	- **Secondary correctives** (if applicable): <list>
289	
290	## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity
291	
292	Independent of any route verdict.
293	
294	- **File count**: <N> (cap: <C>)
295	- **Ready but not dispatched**: <list of files with complete chapters and open sorries that are NOT in the proposal, or "none identified">
296	- **Over the cap**: yes | no
297	- **Under-dispatch finding**: yes | no — <if yes: gap size, how many consecutive iters>
298	- **Iter-over-iter trend**: <e.g. "1 → 1 → 1; consistently under-dispatching 3 ready files">
299	- **Verdict**: OK | OVER_CAP | UNDER_DISPATCH | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS
300	  - OK: dispatch list is within cap, not under-dispatching ready files, and not growing while routes churn.
301	  - OVER_CAP: more files than the cap allows.
302	  - UNDER_DISPATCH: ≥3 ready files absent from the proposal, or consistently fewer than ready across ≥2 iters. Land in must-fix-this-iter.
303	  - BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS: file count growing iter over iter while route signals say CHURNING/STUCK.
304	
305	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if no CHURNING/STUCK verdicts AND no OVER_BUDGET/OVER_CAP/UNDER_DISPATCH/BLOAT findings -->
306	
307	Every CHURNING and every STUCK verdict lands here automatically, every OVER_BUDGET throughput finding (with `Iters left > 0`), and every OVER_CAP / UNDER_DISPATCH / BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS dispatch verdict. Do not under-classify.
308	
309	- Route <name>: <verdict> — primary corrective: <action>. Why: <one line>.
310	- Route <name>: OVER_BUDGET — STRATEGY.md estimates <K> iters, elapsed <K'>. Revise the estimate or escalate.
311	- Route <name>: avoidance pattern — <specific pattern detected>. Primary corrective: <action>.
312	- Dispatch: UNDER_DISPATCH — <N> ready files absent from proposal for <M> consecutive iters. Fill all ready lanes this iter.
313	- Dispatch: OVER_CAP — planner listed <N> files (cap <C>). Re-prioritize; defer <N-C> files.
314	- Dispatch: BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS — file count <a> → <b> → <c> while routes <X>, <Y> remain CHURNING.
315	
316	## Informational <!-- omit if every route is CONVERGING with no commentary worth surfacing -->
317

319	
320	## Overall verdict
321	
322	One paragraph: how many routes are healthy, how many are stuck or churning or avoidance-stalled, what the planner's iter should look like to address the stuck ones. If avoidance patterns were detected, name them explicitly — "the planner has dispatched a single prover for 4 consecutive iters while 3 files were ready" must appear in the verdict so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
323	```

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
cross-domain-inspiration

## Slug
pullback-monoidal

## Structural problem

We need to prove that the **inverse-image (pullback) functor on sheaves of
modules over a scheme is strong monoidal**. Concretely, for a morphism of
schemes `f : Y ⟶ X` and the pullback functor
`f^* : SheafOfModules(𝒪_X) ⥤ SheafOfModules(𝒪_Y)`, we need the two canonical
comparison maps to be **isomorphisms**:

1. `f^*(A ⊗ B) ≅ f^*A ⊗ f^*B`   (tensor comparison)
2. `f^*(𝟙_) ≅ 𝟙_`               (unit comparison: `f^*𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_Y`)

The abstract obstruction: `f^*` is defined in Mathlib as a **left adjoint**
(`(pushforward f).leftAdjoint`); underlyingly the presheaf pullback
`PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is also `(pushforward φ).leftAdjoint`, an abstract
left adjoint with **no sectionwise and no stalkwise value formula** in Mathlib at
the pinned commit. So "compute the value of `f^*` on a section and attach a
tensorator" does not typecheck — there is no concrete sectionwise functor to
attach a tensorator to.

The categorical core, stripped of geometry: **a left adjoint `L ⊣ R` where `R`
is lax monoidal (so `L` is oplax monoidal, giving comparison maps `L(A⊗B) →
LA⊗LB` and `L𝟙 → 𝟙` for free via `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`); we
need to prove these oplax comparison maps are ISOMORPHISMS** (i.e. `L` is strong
monoidal), in a setting where `L` has no explicit value formula but the ambient
categories are sheaf/presheaf categories with a "restrict to an open / take
stalk" family of conservative functors, and the comparison maps ARE
known-iso after restricting to suitable local charts.

## Failed approaches
- Sectionwise `(extendScalars f).Monoidal` tensorator: `extendScalars` is strong
  monoidal in `ModuleCat`, but it is never definitionally the value of the
  abstract presheaf-pullback left adjoint, so there is no sectionwise
  `(pullback φ).obj` to attach the `extendScalars` tensorator to. Structurally
  blocked.
- `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` gives the comparison MAP `δ` for free,
  but only at the PRESHEAF level (there is no `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules …)`
  in Mathlib). Proving `IsIso δ` via `isIso_of_isIso_app` reduces to a
  sectionwise identification of the abstract pullback with `extendScalars` = the
  same missing value formula.
- `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f` (the canonical `f^*𝒪_X → 𝒪_Y` map) is
  an iso only under `F.Final` (open immersions); FALSE for general `f`.

## What already WORKS in the project (the technique to generalize)
The project has an axiom-clean proof of `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`
(`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean:156-193`): for general `f`,
pullback preserves "locally `≅ 𝒪`". Its mechanism is **local-chart finality**:
for a point `y`, pick an affine chart `V ⊆ Y`, factor `V.ι ≫ f` through a local
map `g = f.resLE U V` whose `Opens.map g.base` is `Final` (via
`final_of_representablyFlat`), and then `pullbackObjUnitToUnit g` IS an iso
because `g` is "final". The global comparison map, restricted to `V`, is
identified with the local `pullbackObjUnitToUnit g` through the naturality of
`restrictFunctorIsoPullback` / `pullbackComp` / `pullbackCongr`. The project
also has `isIso_of_isIso_restrict`
(`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:567`, axiom-clean): a
`Scheme.Modules` morphism that is an iso after restricting to every chart of an
open cover is a global iso (stalkwise route).

So the SHAPE that works is: **(comparison map iso) ⟸ (comparison map iso on
every local chart) ⟸ (on a chart, the restricted map equals the local map for a
FINAL morphism, which is iso by the finality instance)**. The missing pieces are
the NATURALITY lemmas tying the restricted global comparison map to the local
one (`pullbackObjUnitToUnit` vs `pullbackComp`/`restrictFunctorIsoPullback`), and
the analogous chain for the TENSOR comparison (where no canonical sheaf-level
comparison map even exists yet — only the presheaf oplax `δ`).

## Search radius
narrow

(Stay within category theory / algebraic geometry / topos theory, but cross
sub-areas. The analogue is likely "one shelf over": some other place in Mathlib
where a left adjoint / inverse-image functor is shown strong monoidal, or where
an oplax-monoidal comparison map is proven iso by reduction to local/stalk
charts, or where pullback of sheaves is shown to commute with tensor.)

## Hints (use sparingly — find what we can't)
Concretely answer, if you can, these three questions (each with a real
`Mathlib.X.Y` path + line citation, verified via the LSP — do NOT allude):
1. Does Mathlib ANYWHERE prove that an inverse-image / pullback functor (of
   sheaves of modules, quasi-coherent sheaves, presheaves of modules, or
   sheaves of abelian groups) commutes with the tensor product, or is strong
   monoidal? Search `PresheafOfModules`, `SheafOfModules`, `QuasicoherentSheaf`,
   and the `CategoryTheory.Sites` pullback/inverse-image API.
2. Is there a Mathlib lemma proving an `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`
   comparison map (or `OplaxMonoidalFunctor`/`Functor.OplaxMonoidal` unit/tensor
   comparison) is an iso under some hypothesis — i.e. a packaged "oplax ⇒ strong
   under condition X" result we could instantiate?
3. Is there a precedent for proving a sheaf-morphism is an iso by reduction to a
   FINAL covering functor (the `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` is-iso-when-Final pattern
   already used in `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`) — i.e. a general
   "iso-after-restriction-to-each-of-a-final-cover ⇒ iso" lemma, OR the relevant
   naturality lemmas for `pullbackComp` / `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` /
   `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` that the local-chart route needs?

The deliverable that helps most: a ranked list of concrete techniques (with
citations) for proving the tensor + unit pullback comparison maps are isos,
ordered by porting cost, PLUS an honest "Mathlib has nothing; the project must
build the naturality cluster from scratch (NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL)" if that is
the truth.

## Design question

The file `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean` is trying to prove
`pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` / `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (Stacks 02KH,
the `i = 0` flat base change of `Rⁱf_*`). The current route reduces to a residual
`hloc(a)`: showing that, for an **affine** morphism `Spec φ : Spec R' ⟶ Spec R`
(`φ : R ⟶ R'` a ring hom) and a module `M` over `R'`, the pushforward-side
restriction map of the pushed-forward tilde sheaf `(Spec φ)_* (M^~)` is an
`IsLocalizedModule (powers a)` localization. This route keeps hitting (4th iter)
a **carrier wall**: viewing the `R'`-module sections as `R`-modules via
`Module.compHom _ φ.hom` is not picked up by `LinearMap.restrictScalars R`
(instance-not-found / defeq-not-syntactic mismatch against the
`modulesSpecToSheaf`-supplied `R`-action and the `restrictScalars` functor's
module instance).

**The real question:** does Mathlib already have a direct, idiomatic statement
that **the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along an AFFINE morphism is
quasi-coherent** (equivalently: `(Spec φ)_* (M^~) ≅ (the tilde of M viewed as an
R-module via φ)^~`, the "affine pushforward = tilde of restriction-of-scalars
module" dictionary), which would let us conclude the localization /
quasi-coherence of the pushforward WITHOUT the manual `hloc` per-`D(a)`
`IsLocalizedModule` transport that keeps hitting the carrier wall?

Equivalently: is there a Mathlib idiom for the whole goal
`affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (base change of `f_*` for `i=0` along a flat
base change of affines) that avoids reconstructing the localization by hand?

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean:~395` — `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule` (the conditional, axiom-clean).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean:~470` — `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (open sorry).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean:~535` — `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` / `hloc` (the carrier-wall residual).
- New axiom-clean bricks just built: `gammaPushforwardIsoAt` (open-indexed `Γ((Spec φ)_* N, U) ≅ restrictScalars φ (Γ(N, (Spec φ)⁻¹ U))`), `tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule`.

## Why now
This lane is STUCK (4 iters, same carrier wall). Before spending a 5th iter on
the manual `IsLocalizedModule` transport, I need to know whether Mathlib has a
higher-level result (affine pushforward preserves quasi-coherence / the
`(Spec φ)_* (M^~) ≅ (restrictScalars φ M)^~` dictionary) that makes the manual
`hloc` discharge unnecessary — i.e. a route pivot, not another carrier-wall
attempt.

## Hints (optional)
Search for, with real `Mathlib.X.Y:line` citations verified via LSP:
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.IsAffine` / `IsAffineHom` + pushforward / `f_*`
  preserving `IsQuasicoherent` / quasi-coherence.
- `ModuleCat.tildeFunctor` / `Spec` / `Modules.tilde` and any
  `(Spec φ)_* ∘ tilde ≅ tilde ∘ restrictScalars φ` style dictionary.
- `QuasicoherentSheaf`, `Scheme.Modules`, `Modules.pushforward`, `Spec.map`.
- `IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars`, `algebraMapSubmonoid`,
  `Module.compHom` — and whether Mathlib's idiom for restrict-scalars +
  `IsLocalizedModule` transport uses `IsScalarTower` / `Algebra` instances
  rather than a `Module.compHom` `letI` (the thing that keeps failing).

## Severity expectation
high-stakes

(This decides whether the FlatBaseChange engine lane pivots its whole
decomposition or keeps grinding the carrier wall. A wrong "no idiom exists"
answer costs many iters.)

content:
2560	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2561	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
2562	\end{proof}
2563	
2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable. The
2584	general case instead uses the \emph{left} adjoint --- pullback \(=\) inverse image
2585	\(=\) extension of scalars --- which is strong monoidal for \emph{any} ring map,
2586	with no iso hypothesis: extension of scalars \(- \otimes_R S\) along \(R \to S\)
2587	carries the tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S)
2588	\otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) unconditionally. This is the whole reason the general
2589	lemma is a separate result.
2590	
2591	\begin{lemma}
2592	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2593	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2595	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2596	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2597	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2598	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2599	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2600	  \[
2601	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2602	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2603	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2604	  \]
2605	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2606	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2607	  and all morphisms \(f\).
2608	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2609	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2610	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2611	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).
2612	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2613	  % "\begin{lemma}
2614	  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
2615	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
2616	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
2617	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
2618	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
2619	  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
2620	  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
2621	  % \end{lemma}"
2622	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2623	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2624	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2625	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2626	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
2627	\end{lemma}
2628	
2629	\begin{proof}
2630	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2631	  The proof mirrors the structure of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but routes
2632	  through the \emph{left} adjoint, where no isomorphism hypothesis on the ring map
2633	  is needed.
2634	
2635	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce to the presheaf level).} By
2636	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the substrate tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
2637	  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
2638	  \(M.\mathrm{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathrm{val}\), where
2639	  \(\otimes^{p} = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The pullback
2640	  commutes with sheafification: the genuine Mathlib lemma
2641	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- which is general
2642	  (it holds for any morphism of ring-sheaves, and is already consumed in the
2643	  open-immersion case \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) --- supplies a natural
2644	  isomorphism moving \(f^*\) inside the sheafification. The claim therefore reduces
2645	  to the presheaf-level comparison
2646	  \[
2647	    (\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes^{p} B)
2648	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2649	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr)
2650	      \;\otimes^{p}\;
2651	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B\bigr),
2652	  \]
2653	  where \(\varphi\) is the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of rings
2654	  induced by \(f\), and \(A = M.\mathrm{val}\), \(B = N.\mathrm{val}\).
2655	
2656	  \emph{Step 2 (the presheaf pullback is strong monoidal).} The presheaf-level
2657	  pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is, sectionwise,
2658	  \emph{extension of scalars} along the section ring maps of \(\varphi\) --- the
2659	  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\). Mathlib equips extension of scalars
2660	  along \emph{any} ring homomorphism \(\psi\) with a strong-monoidal structure,
2661	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2662	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}); its
2663	  tensorator is the canonical isomorphism
2664	  \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\),
2665	  valid with \emph{no} hypothesis on \(\psi\). Assembling these sectionwise
2666	  tensorators against the presheaf restriction morphisms furnishes a strong-monoidal
2667	  structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), whose monoidal
2668	  comparison \(\mu\) is precisely the presheaf-level isomorphism required by Step~1.
2669	  Sheafifying \(\mu\) and transporting along the
2670	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) isomorphism of Step~1 yields the asserted
2671	  isomorphism \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\). Naturality in
2672	  \(M, N\) is inherited from the naturality of \(\mu\) and of sheafification.
2673	
2674	  The contrast with \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is exactly the adjoint used:
2675	  there the comparison is produced from the \emph{right} adjoint
2676	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is strong monoidal only
2677	  because the open immersion makes the comparison ring map a (local) isomorphism;
2678	  here the \emph{left} adjoint \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) is strong monoidal for
2679	  every ring map, so the hypothesis of an isomorphism is dropped entirely.
2680	\end{proof}
2681	
2682	\begin{lemma}
2683	[Pullback preserves the structure sheaf]
2684	  \label{lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2685	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackUnitIso}
2686	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2687	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes. The pullback of the structure sheaf
2688	  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
2689	  \[
2690	    f^*\bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
2691	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2692	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,Y.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf},
2693	    \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
2694	    f^* \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2695	  \]
2696	  where \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf} = \mathcal{O}_X\) is
2697	  the monoidal unit of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2698	\end{lemma}
2699	
2700	\begin{proof}
2701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2702	  The monoidal unit is preserved by the strong-monoidal pullback of
2703	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}. At the section level the relevant comparison is the
2704	  unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) of the strong-monoidal extension of scalars
2705	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\): for a ring map
2706	  \(\psi : R \to S\) it is the canonical isomorphism \(R \otimes_R S \cong S\)
2707	  exhibiting that extension of scalars carries the base ring \(R\) (the
2708	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) unit over \(R\)) to \(S\) (the unit over \(S\)). Assembling
2709	  these sectionwise unit comparisons across the presheaf and sheafifying descends to
2710	  the asserted isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
2711	
2712	  A morphism \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit} : f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to
2713	  \mathcal{O}_Y\) already exists in Mathlib (it is consumed in
2714	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\), where it is shown to be an isomorphism for
2715	  an open immersion by a finality argument on the comma category of the open
2716	  cover). For a \emph{general} morphism \(f\) that finality argument is unavailable,
2717	  so whether \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is itself an isomorphism in general
2718	  must be checked separately; if it is not directly available as an iso, the iso is
2719	  obtained instead from the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) unit comparison
2720	  \(\varepsilon\) above, which is an isomorphism for every ring map and hence
2721	  descends to one for every \(f\).
2722	\end{proof}
2723	
2724	\begin{lemma}
2725	[Pullback preserves $\otimes$-invertibility for a general scheme morphism]
2726	  \label{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
2727	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.pullback}

2747	  % \end{lemma}"
2748	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2749	  \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}: the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{L}\) of an
2750	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}\)-module along a morphism of ringed spaces is

2755	% "\begin{proof}

2760	% $f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$

2763	% \end{proof}"
2764	\begin{proof}
2765	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2766	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
2767	  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation. By
2768	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a

# Blueprint Writer Directive — rewrite `sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`

## Chapter
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`

## Scope (ONLY this section)
Section `sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality` (currently ~L2564–L2786), comprising:
- the section intro paragraph (L2567–2589),
- `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` statement + proof (L2591–2680),
- `lem:pullback_unit_iso` statement + proof (L2682–2722),
- `lem:isinvertible_pullback` statement + proof (L2724–2786).

Do NOT touch any other section of the chapter.

## Why this rewrite (the route pivoted)
The current proof sketches for `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` and `lem:pullback_unit_iso`
describe a **route that cannot be formalized** and must be replaced. The iter-239
prover and two iter-240 audits (lean-vs-blueprint-checker-tensorobjsubstrate;
mathlib-analogist `pullback-monoidal`, persistent file `analogies/pullback-monoidal.md`)
established, with live LSP verification:

1. **`(Presheaf|Sheaf)OfModules.pullback` is an ABSTRACT left adjoint**
   (`(pushforward _).leftAdjoint`) with **no sectionwise and no stalkwise value
   formula** in Mathlib at the pinned commit. So the current Step 2 ("the presheaf
   pullback is, sectionwise, extension of scalars; assemble the sectionwise
   `extendScalars` tensorators") **cannot typecheck** — there is no sectionwise
   `(pullback φ).obj` to attach an `extendScalars` tensorator to.
2. **`Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` does NOT exist.** Mathlib has only
   `Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal` (the opposite direction). So the oplax
   comparison maps on the left-adjoint pullback are **not free**; an
   `OplaxMonoidal` structure on `pullback` must be constructed.
3. There is **no Mathlib pullback tensorator** (`pullbackObjTensorToTensor` does
   not exist) and **no `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)`** (the abstract
   `Sheaf.monoidalCategory` is only for a fixed value category, not modules over a
   varying ring).

The **Stacks statements remain the correct targets** — keep the `% SOURCE` /
`% SOURCE QUOTE` / `\textit{Source: …}` blocks verbatim for all three lemmas
(`lemma-tensor-product-pullback`, `lemma-pullback-invertible`). Only the PROOF
METHOD prose changes.

## The new route to write — Route Z (local-chart finality), TWO phases

Rewrite the section intro and the two sub-lemma proofs to describe **Route Z**.
The mathematical content (read `analogies/pullback-monoidal.md` for the exact
Mathlib citations the writer should weave in as `\texttt{…}` names):

**Phase 1 — `lem:pullback_unit_iso` (`f^*𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_Y`), the cheap half.**
This is reachable directly. The canonical Mathlib map
`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f` (the `f^*𝒪_X → 𝒪_Y` comparison) is an
isomorphism whenever the comparison functor is `Final` — Mathlib instance
`SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal`. This is exactly the `i7`
step already used inside the project's axiom-clean `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`
(`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean:156-193`). For a GENERAL `f`,
`pullbackObjUnitToUnit f` is checked to be an iso by reduction to local charts:
on an affine chart `V ⊆ Y`, factor `V.ι ≫ f` through `g = f.resLE U V` (whose
`Opens.map g.base` is `Final` via `final_of_representablyFlat`), so the restricted
comparison matches `pullbackObjUnitToUnit g` which IS an iso by the Final instance;
globalize "iso on every chart ⇒ global iso" via the project's axiom-clean
`isIso_of_isIso_restrict` (`Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:567`). The naturality
glue (`pullbackComp`, `pullbackId`, `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`, `pullbackCongr`)
is the same chain already wired in `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`. State this as the
proof; note the small remaining work is the naturality lemma tying the
restricted global `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` to the local one.

**Phase 2 — `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` (`f^*(M⊗N) ≅ f^*M ⊗ f^*N`), the real gap.**
There is no Mathlib tensorator on `pullback`, so the comparison map must be built.
Describe the route: (a) construct a `pullbackObjTensorToTensor` comparison
`f^*(A⊗B) → f^*A ⊗ f^*B` (the analogue of `pullbackObjUnitToUnit`); (b) prove it
is an isomorphism by the SAME finality chart-chase as Phase 1 — on a Final local
chart the pullback is locally extension-of-scalars, which IS strong monoidal in
`ModuleCat`, so the local comparison is the `extendScalars` tensorator (an iso),
and `isIso_of_isIso_restrict` globalizes; (c) package strong monoidality via the
Mathlib oplax⇒strong wrapper `Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal` (which takes
`[IsIso η]` and `[∀ X Y, IsIso (δ X Y)]` and yields `Monoidal`, with `μIso`/`εIso`
delivering the comparison isos). Note that producing the `OplaxMonoidal` structure
on `pullback` is itself non-free (no `leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`) — it is built as
the mate of `pushforward`'s lax structure, or directly from the presheaf
pullback's concrete extension-of-scalars description on charts. Record that the
already-landed private brick `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`
(`Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean ~L884`, "sheafification is monoidal up to the
unit") is the RHS reconciliation the eventual `pullbackTensorIso` consumes after
moving the pullback inside sheafification via `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`.

**`lem:isinvertible_pullback` (the Stacks composite) — KEEP essentially as is.**
Its proof composes `pullbackTensorIso⁻¹ ≫ f^*e ≫ pullbackUnitIso` on the existing
witness — that is correct and matches the verbatim Stacks proof quote already in
the file. Leave the statement, `\uses`, SOURCE blocks, and the composite proof
intact. (Only adjust surrounding prose if it references the dead "extendScalars
sectionwise" framing.)

## Also note in the intro
- The section intro currently argues "the general case uses the LEFT adjoint
  extendScalars which is strong monoidal sectionwise for any ring map." Replace
  this framing: the left-adjoint pullback has no sectionwise formula, so the
  monoidality is proven by **local-chart finality** (charts where the pullback IS
  locally extension-of-scalars), not by a global sectionwise tensorator. Keep the
  correct high-level point that this is a genuinely separate fact from the
  open-immersion `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
- Add a short note that a **FLAT-restricted** `IsInvertible.pullback` is an
  available fallback if the general Phase-2 build proves intractable: the RPF
  structure maps (projection `π_T`, base changes) are all flat. (One sentence;
  this is a recorded reversing-signal fallback, not the primary route.)

## Hard constraints
- This is an Archon-original proof METHOD (the Stacks proof is by abstract
  pullback-monoidality; our formalization route is local-chart finality). Keep the
  Stacks `% SOURCE`/`% SOURCE QUOTE` blocks (they cite the STATEMENTS, which are
  unchanged); the proof prose is the project's restatement of the formalization
  route and needs no new external source.
- Do NOT add or remove `\leanok` / `\mathlibok` markers (managed by sync_leanok /
  review). Keep the existing `\lean{}` pins on all three lemmas.
- Prose must be mathematical, not Lean tactics. Mathlib names may appear as
  `\texttt{…}` identifiers (e.g. `\texttt{CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal}`), as the
  existing blueprint does.
- Read `analogies/pullback-monoidal.md` first for the precise Mathlib citations.

356	\begin{lemma}
357	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
358	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
359	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
360	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
361	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
362	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
363	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
364	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
365	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
366	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
367	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
368	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
369	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
370	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
371	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
372	  \[
373	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
374	    \;\cong\;
375	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
376	  \]
377	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
378	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
379	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
380	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
381	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
382	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
383	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
384	
385	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
386	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
387	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
388	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
389	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
390	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
391	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
396	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars}
397	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
398	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
399	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
400	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
401	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
402	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
403	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
404	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
405	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
406	
407	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
408	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
409	  \[
410	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
411	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
412	  \]
413	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
414	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
415	
416	  \medskip
417	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
418	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
419	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
420	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
421	
422	  \medskip
423	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
424	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
425	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
426	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
427	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
428	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
429	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
430	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
431	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
432	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
433	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
434	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
435	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
436	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
437	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
438	  an isomorphism.
439	
440	  \medskip
441	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
442	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
443	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
444	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
445	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
446	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
447	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
448	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
449	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
450	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
451	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
452	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
453	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
454	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
455	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
456	
457	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
458	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
459	  isomorphism
460	  \[
461	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
462	      \;\cong\;
463	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
464	  \]
465	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
466	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
467	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
468	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
469	  the \(\top\)-level one of
470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
471	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
472	  \(\top\).
473	
474	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
475	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
476	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
477	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
478	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
479	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
480	  \[
481	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
482	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
483	    \;=\;
484	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
485	  \]
486	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
487	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
488	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
489	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
490	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
491	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).

493	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target

496	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse

498	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that

500	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
501	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
502	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
503	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
504	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
505	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
506	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
508	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
509	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
510	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
511	  global ring map.
512	
513	  \medskip
514	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed

input.old_string:
\emph{(c) Packaging as strong monoidality.} The two comparison isomorphisms --- the
  tensor comparison \(\delta\) just shown and the unit comparison \(\eta = \varepsilon\)
  of \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} --- are exactly the inputs of the Mathlib wrapper
  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal}\), which from an
  \(\mathtt{OplaxMonoidal}\) structure together with \([\mathtt{IsIso}\,\eta]\) and
  \([\forall\,X\,Y,\ \mathtt{IsIso}\,(\delta\,X\,Y)]\) manufactures a
  \(\mathtt{Monoidal}\) (strong) structure on \(f^*\), with \(\mu\mathtt{Iso}\) and
  \(\varepsilon\mathtt{Iso}\) delivering the comparison isomorphisms. Note that the
  underlying \(\mathtt{OplaxMonoidal}\) structure on \(f^*\) is itself \emph{not}
  free (there is no \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) transfer; Mathlib ships only
  \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) for the opposite direction), so it is built by
  hand --- as the mate of the lax structure on \(\mathtt{pushforward}\), or directly
  from the presheaf-level extension-of-scalars description on charts --- before
  feeding the two \(\mathtt{IsIso}\) facts to the wrapper. The resulting \(\mu\mathtt{Iso}\)
  is the asserted isomorphism \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\),
  natural in \(M\) and \(N\).
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
\emph{(c) The pointwise isomorphism is all that is needed.} The sole consumer of
  this lemma on the critical path, \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}, requires only the
  comparison \emph{isomorphism at the single witness pair} \((M, N)\) together with its
  naturality --- \emph{not} a strong-monoidal functor structure on \(f^*\). Parts
  (a)--(b) already deliver exactly that: the comparison
  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) at \((M, N)\), shown to be an isomorphism by the
  finality chart-chase. We therefore take the asserted isomorphism
  \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\) to be \(\mathtt{asIso}\) of that
  pointwise comparison, with naturality inherited from the naturality of the comparison
  and of sheafification.

  Packaging the whole family \(\{\delta_{M,N}\}\) into a strong-monoidal structure on
  \(f^*\) via the Mathlib wrapper \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal}\) (from
  \([\mathtt{IsIso}\,\eta]\) and \([\forall X Y,\ \mathtt{IsIso}\,(\delta_{X,Y})]\)) is
  possible, but it additionally requires a \emph{hand-built} \(\mathtt{OplaxMonoidal}\,f^*\)
  instance --- there is no \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) transfer (Mathlib ships
  only \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), the opposite direction), so the oplax
  structure with all its coherence is not free. That packaging is \emph{not} needed for
  the invertibility consumer and is deferred as off the critical path; the pointwise
  isomorphism above is the deliverable.
\end{proof}

2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
2587	``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
2588	strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
2589	a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
2590	value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2591	tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
2592	ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
2593	structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
2594	oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
2595	Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
2596	finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
2597	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2598	and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
2599	comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
2600	\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
2601	\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
2602	result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
2603	genuinely new tensor comparison).
2604	
2605	If the general tensor comparison should prove intractable to formalize, a
2606	\emph{flat-restricted} version of \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} suffices for the
2607	relative Picard consumer: the structure maps it actually pulls back along --- the
2608	projection \(\pi_T\) and the base-change morphisms of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
2609	--- are all flat, and over a flat ring map the pullback is exact and the tensorator
2610	argument simplifies. This is recorded as a fallback, not the primary route.
The intro is good except the flat-restriction fallback paragraph, which the strategy-critic flagged as illusory. Let me replace it with the honest framing.

input.old_string:
If the general tensor comparison should prove intractable to formalize, a
\emph{flat-restricted} version of \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} suffices for the
relative Picard consumer: the structure maps it actually pulls back along --- the
projection \(\pi_T\) and the base-change morphisms of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
--- are all flat, and over a flat ring map the pullback is exact and the tensorator
argument simplifies. This is recorded as a fallback, not the primary route.
input.new_string:
It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would
simplify nothing here. The only genuinely simpler-looking alternative --- routing
through local triviality, ``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\)
\(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to locally \(\mathcal{O}\)'' --- is a mirage on this
carrier: recovering \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse
bridge \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly
the dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. The pullback-monoidality
route is therefore primary precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse
witness directly, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

# Blueprint Reviewer Directive — ts240 scoped re-review (same-iter fast path)

You audit the whole blueprint as always, but this dispatch exists to re-clear the
HARD GATE for ONE chapter that was rewritten this iter after a must-fix finding.

## Focus chapter (gate decision needed THIS iter)
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`, section
`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality` (the three lemmas `lem:pullback_tensor_iso`,
`lem:pullback_unit_iso`, `lem:isinvertible_pullback`).

## Why
The iter-239 lean-vs-blueprint check flagged `sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`
as **must-fix**: its proof sketches described a route (sectionwise `extendScalars`
tensorator on the abstract left-adjoint `PresheafOfModules.pullback`) that cannot
be formalized (the pullback has no sectionwise/stalkwise value formula). This iter
the section was rewritten to **Route Z = local-chart finality** and `lem:pullback_tensor_iso`
was DESCOPED to a pointwise comparison iso (the full `CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal`
strong-monoidal packaging — which needs a hand-built `OplaxMonoidal` instance, not
free — is explicitly recorded as off the critical path). A prover (`mathlib-build`)
is queued to attack Phase 1 (`pullbackUnitIso`) + the pointwise Phase 2 this iter
IF this chapter clears.

## The question for the focus chapter
Is `sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality` now **complete: true** AND **correct: true**
with **no must-fix-this-iter** finding? Specifically check:
- The proof sketches no longer describe the dead sectionwise-`extendScalars` route;
  the route is now local-chart finality (Phase 1 globalizes the proven
  `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` Final-chart chase via
  `isIso_of_isIso_restrict`; Phase 2 builds `pullbackObjTensorToTensor` and proves
  it iso by the same chart-chase, taken pointwise).
- The descope is coherent: `lem:isinvertible_pullback` (the Stacks composite
  `pullbackTensorIso⁻¹ ≫ f^*e ≫ pullbackUnitIso`) consumes only the pointwise iso,
  and the off-path note about full monoidal packaging is not load-bearing.
- The Mathlib facts cited (`instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal`,
  `final_of_representablyFlat`, `sheafificationCompPullback`,
  `isIso_of_isIso_restrict`) are the right tools and the proof is formalizable as
  written.
- `\uses{}` are acyclic and the Stacks `% SOURCE QUOTE` blocks are intact.

## Also (whole-blueprint, normal pass)
Report your usual per-chapter checklist for the rest of the blueprint, but the
gate decision I act on this iter is for `Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` only.
Note also whether `Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex` (two new pinned blocks
`lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt`, `lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule` + the
natural-in-open notes added to `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`) remains
complete+correct (it cleared the gate last iter; confirm the additive edits did
not regress it).

# Iter-240 plan-agent run

## Headline outcome

The **"both walled lanes get concrete verified pivots → recover with same-iter prover dispatch"** iter.
iter-239 ended with BOTH dispatched lanes route-blocked: the A.1.c substrate `IsInvertible.pullback`
recipe (sectionwise-`extendScalars`) was proven structurally DEAD, and FlatBaseChange's affine close was
STUCK on the 4th recurrence of the `Module.compHom` carrier wall. This iter ran the design pass the review
demanded (mathlib-analogist ×2 + strategy-critic + progress-critic), and BOTH lanes came back with
concrete, LSP-verified pivots — neither is a verbatim re-dispatch:

- **A.1.c substrate → Route Z (local-chart finality).** Phase 1 `pullbackUnitIso` is a cheap globalization
  of the already-proven `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` Final-chart chase; Phase 2
  `pullbackTensorIso` is a **pointwise** comparison iso (descoped per strategy-critic — the full
